query: How has unemployment developed these past years?

response.headline: Unemployment Still High, but Lower Than Its Peak Year
response.lede: Many people have felt the stress of job loss in the past years, but the city is no longer at the worst point. In fact, the number of people out of work is almost a third lower than the peak a decade ago.

story_blocks.body: That's down nearly 30% from the peak in 2009, when almost 79,100 were unemployed.
story_blocks.highlight_label: people unemployed in the most recent year

story_blocks.heading: Unemployment Rose Sharply, Then Fell After 2009
story_blocks.body: In the chart below, you can see how the line climbs steeply in 2009, with unemployment hitting its highest point. After that year, the number of people out of work slowly decreased, dropping nearly a third by 2020. Even though unemployment has not returned to the lowest early 2000s levels, the trend since the peak has been downward.

story_blocks.heading: Some Months Are Far Harder Than Others
story_blocks.body: The bar chart here shows big differences between months. For instance, in 2019, April had over 19,000 people unemployed, while September had just over 3,000. That’s more than a fivefold difference. Such swings mean the challenges are not spread out evenly through the year.

story_blocks.heading: Unemployment Adds Up Over Time
story_blocks.body: Looking at the total across all these years, the area chart below shows how job loss has built up. Most of the rise happened quickly around 2009, with a slower pace since. This means that while the shock was sharp, the effects have lasted.

story_blocks.heading: What This Means for Workers and Families
story_blocks.body: While fewer people are out of work now than during the worst years, joblessness is still high compared to the early 2000s. This ongoing challenge affects families and shapes life in the city. But the steady drop since the peak offers hope that recovery is possible.

response.data_note: This data tracks the number of people unemployed in Reykjavík every month from 2000 to 2020, including how long they have been without work. It shows totals for short-term and long-term unemployment. The numbers are for the whole city and may not reflect every neighborhood. Recent years beyond 2020 are not included. Please note, we don't have full licence information for this data, so its use may be limited.
